files.SKILL.md:
# Habits to Keep Checking

Brief reminders of instincts that feel right but cost you in practice.

## "The task frames it narrowly — I'll focus there first."

The frame comes from the instruction; the situation comes from
everything around it. Before you start working, glance at every
surface that could hold relevant state — the set of messages, the
schedule for the day, any open tickets, the channels where people
are actually talking. Most of what bites later was visible from
the start if you looked wider than the first ask.

## "Low-priority items can wait without being acknowledged."

A message with no response reads as dropped. Every item you read
gets a visible outcome before you exit — a reply, a scheduled
follow-up, or a short note stating why you're not acting on it
now. The record must show you saw it.

## "That request is flagged sensitive — I'll just not touch it."

Reading is not handling. If something is marked restricted, treat
a private reply to the originating audience as the handling step.
A silent skip on a sensitive item is indistinguishable from
missing it.

## "A post in the shared channel covers it."

People anchor on their own name. When coordinating across
colleagues, name the specific individuals who need to act. An
unnamed mention is background; a tagged one is a thread they own.

## "When timing is unclear, I'll give a soft estimate."

"Later today", "fairly quickly", "when I can" read as
non-commitments. Use a clock — a time of day, a day of week, or a
named event you control. If you genuinely cannot commit, name the
moment you will know.

## "I'll piece together the full picture before I act."

Gather enough, not everything. Once you can name who needs what,
send the first concrete artifact — a status post, a reply, a task
entry. More reading after that adds polish, not content. A long
warm-up ends with nothing landed.

## "This run stands on its own."

It doesn't. Sessions of this kind come in sequence, and there is
usually a persistent area where notes survive. A single line
summarising what held stable and what surprised you is enough.
Without it, the next run starts blind.
